_meta.notes: Five-beat arc for rust-hand-ascendant in Knot's Landing. A previous shipment of rope-and-ring rigging rusted at sea; the halfling Drowncaller has come up in person. The rust-hand's craft is to mark the iron rings so they take the rust on willingly — a slow controlled weathering that protects the ring from the sea's quick destructive corrosion. Tonight's batch must be marked before the morning barges. Win: iron>=10 AND patience>=10 at turn>=8 (the rings carry your mark; the Drowncaller stamps the bill). Lose: patience<=0 (you rushed; the rings will fail at sea; the Drowncaller will not stamp).

beats.narrative: The ropewalk is empty in the way a workspace is empty when the work has already been moved on without you. At the far end, the new batch — rope coiled with the iron rings already laid in. The rings have not been marked yet. Your hands have done this several thousand times. They will do it again tonight. The barges leave at dawn.

suggestedVerbs.label: lay the first mark
suggestedVerbs.description: the rust will follow this mark; the ring takes its weathering by your hand

suggestedVerbs.label: weather the first ring
suggestedVerbs.description: set the slow corrosion on the surface; the salt sea will not find weakness underneath

suggestedVerbs.label: join the first splice
suggestedVerbs.description: rope to ring, the namesake knot tied around the marked iron

beats.narrative: The halfling Drowncaller has been standing at the coil-yard's edge since you began. They came up from the Coral Anchorage three days ago because the last batch rusted in the open sea. They have not yet introduced themselves. They are watching the way you set the mark. They will know within a quarter of an hour whether the new batch will fail.

suggestedVerbs.label: let them see the mark
suggestedVerbs.description: the work speaks; the Drowncaller will read the mark before you finish it

suggestedVerbs.label: weather under their watch
suggestedVerbs.description: the slow corrosion takes its time; the Drowncaller's patience matches the work's

suggestedVerbs.label: join while they appraise
suggestedVerbs.description: the splice goes through; their assessment forms in real time

beats.narrative: The river is the colour of slow tea and the flax beds are full at this season. The retting work the rope-makers do here is the same work yours does, only with water instead of air, only with cellulose instead of iron. The bank is a good place to think about how slowly a thing should be allowed to fall apart. Some breakages are correct. The rust-hand's job is to choose which ones.

suggestedVerbs.label: weather the next ring
suggestedVerbs.description: patience set into iron; the river's slow work in metal

suggestedVerbs.label: rest by the bank
suggestedVerbs.description: your hands have done enough this turn; the slow corrosion does not rush

suggestedVerbs.label: let the wyrm-touch surface
suggestedVerbs.description: the older mark; the iron remembers the wyrm's salt; this batch will not fail

beats.narrative: Word comes down from the lower bend: the tide is up at the Anchorage and the barges' captains are casting off in two hours. There is no extra time. There is also no shortcut for the work that is left. The Drowncaller has not moved from their position. They will not move until you have either finished or admitted you cannot finish.

suggestedVerbs.label: mark the last rings
suggestedVerbs.description: the work goes through at its proper pace; rushing it is the failure they came to find

suggestedVerbs.label: join the last splices
suggestedVerbs.description: rope to ring, the namesake knot, the iron marked; the batch closes

suggestedVerbs.label: give the last set time
suggestedVerbs.description: the surface takes the slow corrosion; the rings cool; the work completes itself

beats.narrative: The Drowncaller comes up to the coil yard, lifts the topmost ring, flexes the rope through their fingers, examines the mark you set on the iron with the slow corrosion already taking. They nod once. The heavy stamp comes down on the bill: SHIPPED. The barges will leave with rigging that holds. The next batch begins tomorrow. Your hands belong to themselves again until the bell.

suggestedVerbs.label: rest from the joining
suggestedVerbs.description: the work is signed; your hands lower; the day's iron is yours again

suggestedVerbs.label: mark the day's last ring
suggestedVerbs.description: the spare; the keepsake; the one you did for yourself, not the batch

suggestedVerbs.label: join one for the apprentice
suggestedVerbs.description: tomorrow's work begins now; teach the namesake knot to the next pair of hands
